title: External Validation with CAPS-5 Data
text: CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale DSM-5) gold standard PTSD diagnosis. PCL-5 CAPS-5 data available participants, optimised PCL-5 symptom combinations can evaluated clinician-rated diagnosis. CAPS-5 mirrors PCL-5 structure: 20 items 0–4 severity scale, clinician integrates frequency intensity single rating. PTSDdiag functions work identically CAPS-5 data columns renamed. vignette demonstrates : Prepare CAPS-5 data analysis Apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria CAPS-5 data Compare PCL-5 CAPS-5 diagnoses using either instrument reference standard Evaluate optimised PCL-5 combinations CAPS-5 gold standard single-dataset PCL-5 workflow, see Full Internal Analysis vignette. cross-sample validation using two PCL-5 datasets, see External Validation (PCL-5) vignette.

title: External Validation with CAPS-5 Data
text: use included simulated_ptsd dataset PCL-5 sample. illustrate workflow, simulate CAPS-5 data adding clinician variance PCL-5 scores. practice, come separate instruments administered participants.
code: library("PTSDdiag") data("simulated_ptsd") simulated_ptsd_renamed <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) set.seed(42) caps5_raw <- simulated_ptsd for (j in seq_len(ncol(caps5_raw))) {   noise <- sample(c(-1L, 0L, 0L, 0L, 1L), nrow(caps5_raw), replace = TRUE)   caps5_raw[[j]] <- pmin(4L, pmax(0L, caps5_raw[[j]] + noise)) }

title: External Validation with PCL-5 Data
text: central question developing simplified diagnostic criteria whether combinations optimised one sample generalise different population. PTSDdiag supports derivation-validation framework : Optimal symptom combinations derived clinical sample (e.g., veterans high PTSD prevalence) combinations validated independent sample (e.g., general population lower prevalence) vignette demonstrates workflow using two simulated datasets included package: simulated_ptsd: clinical veteran sample (N = 5,000, ~94% PTSD prevalence) simulated_ptsd_genpop: general population sample (N = 1,200, ~21% PTSD prevalence) use optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() find 10 best six-symptom combinations veteran data, apply general population data using apply_symptom_combinations(). single-dataset workflow (optimisation internal validation), see Full Internal Analysis vignette. validation CAPS-5 gold standard, see External Validation (CAPS-5) vignette.

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: PTSDdiag package provides tools analysing optimising PTSD diagnostic criteria using PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist DSM-5) data. vignette demonstrates complete single-dataset workflow: Import prepare PCL-5 data Calculate descriptive statistics reliability metrics Identify optimal symptom combinations (hierarchical non-hierarchical) Apply ICD-11 PTSD diagnostic criteria compare diagnostic systems Export import optimised combinations Validate models using holdout cross-validation workflows involving independent validation datasets, see External Validation (PCL-5) External Validation (CAPS-5) vignettes.

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: Install released version CRAN: development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("PTSDdiag") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("TobiasRSpiller/PTSDdiag")

title: Full Internal Analysis
code: library("PTSDdiag") library(psych)     # For reliability analysis

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: package includes simulated_ptsd, dataset 5,000 simulated PCL-5 records. record rates 20 PTSD symptoms 5-point scale (0 = , 1 = little bit, 2 = Moderately, 3 = Quite bit, 4 = Extremely). PCL-5 organises symptoms four DSM-5 clusters: Symptoms 1–5: Criterion B (Intrusion) Symptoms 6–7: Criterion C (Avoidance) Symptoms 8–14: Criterion D (Negative alterations cognitions mood) Symptoms 15–20: Criterion E (Alterations arousal reactivity) Input data must contain numeric values 0–4 , missing values, 20 columns (one per symptom), row-wise observations.

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: package functions expect columns named symptom_1 symptom_20. Use rename_ptsd_columns() standardise:
code: simulated_ptsd_renamed <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) names(simulated_ptsd_renamed) #>  [1] "symptom_1"  "symptom_2"  "symptom_3"  "symptom_4"  "symptom_5"  #>  [6] "symptom_6"  "symptom_7"  "symptom_8"  "symptom_9"  "symptom_10" #> [11] "symptom_11" "symptom_12" "symptom_13" "symptom_14" "symptom_15" #> [16] "symptom_16" "symptom_17" "symptom_18" "symptom_19" "symptom_20"

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: calculate total scores, apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, summarise:
code: # Calculate total scores (range 0-80) simulated_ptsd_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(simulated_ptsd_renamed)  # Apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(simulated_ptsd_total)  # Summary statistics summarize_ptsd(simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: Cronbach’s alpha assesses internal consistency PCL-5 items:
code: cronbach <- psych::alpha(subset(simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed, select = -total)) round(cronbach$total, 3) #>  raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r    S/N   ase  mean    sd median_r #>      0.913     0.916   0.921     0.341 10.851 0.002 2.796 0.594    0.332

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: Standard PTSD diagnosis requires evaluating 20 symptoms across four clusters. goal identify reduced symptom combinations preserve polythetic structure diagnosis maintaining diagnostic accuracy. examine two structural approaches: hierarchical approach (requiring least one symptom DSM-5 cluster) non-hierarchical approach (selecting symptoms purely accuracy). Note computation time. examples use 4-symptom combinations (C(20, 4) = 4,845 evaluate), run seconds. typical analysis uses 6-symptom combinations (C(20, 6) = 38,760), takes 1–2 minutes 5,000 observations.

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: hierarchical approach requires least one symptom DSM-5 cluster, maintaining structural logic diagnostic criteria. score_by argument controls optimisation target: "newly_nondiagnosed" – minimise false negatives (missed diagnoses) "false_cases" – minimise total misclassifications function returns three elements. 1. best symptom combinations: 2. row-level comparison DSM-5-TR vs. combination (first 5 rows): Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows) 3. Diagnostic accuracy metrics: Hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics
code: best_combinations_hierarchical <- optimize_combinations_clusters(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   clusters   = list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20),   show_progress = FALSE ) best_combinations_hierarchical$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  7 11 17 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  4  6 11 17 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  1  6 11 17 knitr::kable(   head(best_combinations_hierarchical$diagnosis_comparison, 5),   caption = "Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows)" ) knitr::kable(   best_combinations_hierarchical$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: non-hierarchical approach selects symptoms purely accuracy, without requiring cluster representation. Best symptom combinations: Diagnosis comparison (first 5 rows): Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top non-hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows) Diagnostic accuracy metrics: Non-hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics
code: best_combinations_nonhierarchical <- optimize_combinations(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   show_progress = FALSE ) best_combinations_nonhierarchical$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  6  7 13 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  4  6  7 13 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  4  6  7 17 knitr::kable(   head(best_combinations_nonhierarchical$diagnosis_comparison, 5),   caption = "Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top non-hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows)" ) knitr::kable(   best_combinations_nonhierarchical$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Non-hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: Optimised combinations can benchmarked established reference standards: full DSM-5-TR criteria ICD-11 PTSD definition.

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: ICD-11 defines PTSD using narrower symptom set DSM-5-TR. Using PCL-5 items, ICD-11 requires three following clusters met (symptom present = score >= 2): create_icd11_diagnosis() applies criteria: Diagnostic accuracy ICD-11 relative DSM-5-TR: ICD-11 vs. DSM-5-TR: diagnostic accuracy metrics
code: icd11_result <- create_icd11_diagnosis(simulated_ptsd_renamed) head(icd11_result) #>   PTSD_orig PTSD_icd11 #> 1      TRUE       TRUE #> 2      TRUE       TRUE #> 3      TRUE      FALSE #> 4      TRUE       TRUE #> 5      TRUE       TRUE #> 6      TRUE       TRUE cat(sprintf("DSM-5-TR prevalence: %.1f%%\n", mean(icd11_result$PTSD_orig)  * 100)) #> DSM-5-TR prevalence: 94.2% cat(sprintf("ICD-11 prevalence:   %.1f%%\n", mean(icd11_result$PTSD_icd11) * 100)) #> ICD-11 prevalence:   92.8% knitr::kable(   create_readable_summary(summarize_ptsd_changes(icd11_result)),   digits  = 4,   caption = "ICD-11 vs. DSM-5-TR: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: compare_diagnostic_systems() combines number diagnostic criteria single table. compare DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, sets optimised combinations: Comparison: DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, non-hierarchical, hierarchical combinations Use labels argument rename optimised combination rows (default, column names symptom_1_6_8_15 used). Set icd11 = FALSE omit ICD-11 row.
code: tbl_full <- compare_diagnostic_systems(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   best_combinations_nonhierarchical$diagnosis_comparison,   best_combinations_hierarchical$diagnosis_comparison,   icd11 = TRUE )  knitr::kable(   tbl_full,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Comparison: DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, non-hierarchical, and hierarchical combinations" )

title: Full Internal Analysis
text: k-fold cross-validation provides robust estimate generalisation repeating train/test split k times. Results fold – non-hierarchical: Cross-validation: non-hierarchical results fold Results fold – hierarchical: Cross-validation: hierarchical results fold Stable combinations across folds – combination appeared multiple folds, average performance reported: Stable non-hierarchical combinations across folds Stable hierarchical combinations across folds
code: cv_results <- cross_validation(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   k          = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   seed       = 123,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3 ) knitr::kable(   cv_results$without_clusters$summary_by_fold,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Cross-validation: non-hierarchical results by fold" ) knitr::kable(   cv_results$with_clusters$summary_by_fold,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Cross-validation: hierarchical results by fold" ) if (!is.null(cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   knitr::kable(     cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary,     digits  = 4,     caption = "Stable non-hierarchical combinations across folds"   ) } else {   cat("No combination appeared in more than one fold (non-hierarchical).\n") } if (!is.null(cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   knitr::kable(     cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary,     digits  = 4,     caption = "Stable hierarchical combinations across folds"   ) } else {   cat("No combination appeared in more than one fold (hierarchical).\n") }

title: Quick Start

code: df <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) df <- calculate_ptsd_total(df) df <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(df)  summarize_ptsd(df) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: Quick Start
text: optimize_combinations() searches best k-symptom combinations (: 4 20) preserve DSM-5-TR diagnostic accuracy. Setting score_by = "newly_nondiagnosed" minimises missed diagnoses (false negatives). Note computation time. example uses n_symptoms = 4 (4,845 combinations) speed. typical research analysis uses n_symptoms = 6 (38,760 combinations), takes 1–2 minutes. Top combinations found: Diagnostic accuracy metrics: Top 3 non-hierarchical combinations
code: result <- optimize_combinations(   rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd),   n_symptoms    = 4,   n_required    = 3,   n_top         = 3,   score_by      = "newly_nondiagnosed",   show_progress = FALSE ) result$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  6  7 13 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  4  6  7 13 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  4  6  7 17 knitr::kable(result$summary, digits = 4,              caption = "Top 3 non-hierarchical combinations")

title: Find optimal non-hierarchical six-symptom combinations for PTSD diagnosis — analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required
text: function: Tests possible combinations 6 symptoms 20 PCL-5 items Requires 4 symptoms present (>=2 original 0-4 scale) diagnosis Identifies three combinations best match original DSM-5 diagnosis Optimization can based either: Minimizing false cases (false positives false negatives) Minimizing false negatives (newly non-diagnosed cases) symptom clusters PCL-5 : Items 1-5: Intrusion symptoms (Criterion B) Items 6-7: Avoidance symptoms (Criterion C) Items 8-14: Negative alterations cognitions mood (Criterion D) Items 15-20: Alterations arousal reactivity (Criterion E)

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
text: Applies one pre-specified symptom combinations new dataset returns comparison dataframe baseline DSM-5 diagnosis simplified diagnosis combination. function enables external validation allowing combinations derived one dataset tested another.

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
code: apply_symptom_combinations(data, combinations, n_required = 4, clusters = NULL)

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
text: data dataframe containing exactly 20 columns PCL-5 item scores   (output rename_ptsd_columns). symptom scored   0-4 scale : 0 = 1 = little bit 2 = Moderately 3 = Quite bit 4 = Extremely combinations list integer vectors. vector contains symptom indices (e.g., c(4, 6, 7, 17, 19, 20)). vectors must length. Typically obtained results$best_symptoms optimization run. n_required Integer specifying many symptoms must present (scored >= 2 original scale) positive diagnosis (default: 4). Must 1 length combination. clusters NULL (default) non-hierarchical checking,   named list integer vectors defining cluster structure   hierarchical checking. provided, positive diagnosis additionally   requires present symptoms span defined clusters. PCL-5:   list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20)

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
text: dataframe columns: PTSD_orig: Logical. Full DSM-5 diagnosis computed data. One column per combination (logical): Simplified diagnosis     combination. Column names follow symptom_X_Y_Z pattern. dataframe can passed directly summarize_ptsd_changes compute diagnostic accuracy metrics.

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
text: function: Computes baseline DSM-5 diagnosis (PTSD_orig)     provided data using binarized scores Binarizes symptom data (scores >= 2 become 1, others become 0) combination, determines diagnosis based whether least     n_required symptoms present clusters provided, additionally checks present     symptoms span defined clusters (hierarchical checking) Returns comparison dataframe suitable     summarize_ptsd_changes Typical workflow external validation: Derive optimal combinations Dataset using     optimize_combinations     optimize_combinations_clusters Apply combinations Dataset B using function Compute diagnostic metrics using     summarize_ptsd_changes     create_readable_summary

title: Apply pre-specified symptom combinations to new data — apply_symptom_combinations
code: # Create example data set.seed(42) ptsd_data <- data.frame(matrix(sample(0:4, 400, replace=TRUE), ncol=20)) names(ptsd_data) <- paste0("symptom_", 1:20)  # Define some combinations to test my_combinations <- list(   c(1, 6, 8, 10, 15, 19),   c(2, 7, 9, 11, 16, 20) )  # Apply combinations (non-hierarchical) comparison <- apply_symptom_combinations(ptsd_data, my_combinations,                 n_required = 4)  # Compute metrics metrics <- summarize_ptsd_changes(comparison) create_readable_summary(metrics) #>                 Scenario Total Diagnosed Total Non-Diagnosed True Positive #> 1              PTSD_orig        11 (55%)             9 (45%)            11 #> 2 symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19         9 (45%)            11 (55%)             8 #> 3 symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20         9 (45%)            11 (55%)             7 #>   True Negative Newly Diagnosed Newly Non-Diagnosed True Cases False Cases #> 1             9               0                   0         20           0 #> 2             8               1                   3         16           4 #> 3             7               2                   4         14           6 #>   Sensitivity Specificity    PPV    NPV #> 1      1.0000      1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 #> 2      0.7273      0.8889 0.8889 0.7273 #> 3      0.6364      0.7778 0.7778 0.6364  # Apply with hierarchical checking pcl5_clusters <- list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20) hier_comparison <- apply_symptom_combinations(ptsd_data, my_combinations,                      n_required = 4, clusters = pcl5_clusters) summarize_ptsd_changes(hier_comparison) #>                                        column diagnosed non_diagnosed #> PTSD_orig                           PTSD_orig        11             9 #> symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19 symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19         1            19 #> symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20 symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20         3            17 #>                        newly_diagnosed newly_nondiagnosed true_positive #> PTSD_orig                            0                  0            11 #> symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19               0                 10             1 #> symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20               0                  8             3 #>                        true_negative true_cases false_cases sensitivity #> PTSD_orig                          9         20           0  1.00000000 #> symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19             9         10          10  0.09090909 #> symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20             9         12           8  0.27272727 #>                        specificity ppv       npv diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig                        1   1 1.0000000                55 #> symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19           1   1 0.4736842                 5 #> symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20           1   1 0.5294118                15 #>                        non_diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig                                 45 #> symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19                    95 #> symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20                    85

what: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
title: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
text: Produces single unified summary table comparing diagnostic performance multiple criteria chosen reference standard. Suitable use manuscript table comparing optimized symptom combinations, ICD-11, CAPS-5, DSM-5-TR one kable-ready output.

title: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
code: compare_diagnostic_systems(   data,   ...,   icd11 = TRUE,   caps5_data = NULL,   reference = c("pcl5", "caps5"),   labels = NULL )

title: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
text: data dataframe containing exactly 20 columns PCL-5 item scores (output rename_ptsd_columns). Always required. Used compute PCL-5 DSM-5-TR diagnosis , icd11 = TRUE, ICD-11 diagnosis. ... Zero comparison dataframes, containing PTSD_orig column least one additional logical column representing diagnostic system (e.g. output apply_symptom_combinations). caps5_data NULL, PTSD_orig columns must identical one computed data. caps5_data provided, row counts validated. icd11 Logical. TRUE (default), compute ICD-11 PTSD diagnosis data include row output. caps5_data Optional dataframe containing exactly 20 columns CAPS-5 item severity scores (output rename_caps5_columns). Must number rows data (paired participants). provided, CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR diagnosis computed internally included comparison. reference Character. DSM-5-TR diagnosis serves reference standard: "pcl5" (default) "caps5". reference row always sensitivity = specificity = 1 zero misclassifications. Setting reference = "caps5" requires caps5_data provided. labels Optional character vector display names systems coming ..., order columns appear across ... inputs (excluding PTSD_orig columns). apply built-rows (DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, CAPS-5), always labelled automatically. NULL (default), column names used. warning issued length match.

title: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
text: data.frame one row per diagnostic system   following columns: system: Display name diagnostic criterion n_diagnosed: Number cases meeting criterion pct_diagnosed: Percentage total sample diagnosed (2 dp) sensitivity: 4 dp specificity: 4 dp ppv: Positive predictive value, 4 dp npv: Negative predictive value, 4 dp n_false_negative: Cases missed vs. reference pct_false_negative: Percentage total sample, 2 dp n_false_positive: Cases -diagnosed vs. reference pct_false_positive: Percentage total sample, 2 dp n_misclassified: Total misclassified cases

title: Compare multiple diagnostic systems against a reference standard — compare_diagnostic_systems
text: function: Computes PCL-5 DSM-5-TR diagnosis data caps5_data provided, computes CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR     diagnosis Sets reference standard based reference: either     PCL-5 CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR diagnosis. reference row always appears     first sensitivity = specificity = 1. Optionally computes ICD-11 diagnosis data     icd11 = TRUE Collects non-PTSD_orig columns ...     comparison dataframes (e.g. output     apply_symptom_combinations) Calls summarize_ptsd_changes internally reshapes     result presentation-ready table caps5_data NULL (default), labels follow original convention: "DSM-5-TR" "ICD-11". caps5_data provided, labels disambiguated instrument name: "DSM-5-TR (PCL-5)", "DSM-5-TR (CAPS-5)", "ICD-11 (PCL-5)". caps5_data provided, strict PTSD_orig validation ... inputs relaxed row-count check , comparison dataframes may derived either PCL-5 CAPS-5 data (produce different PTSD_orig vectors).

title: Compute CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnosis — create_caps5_diagnosis
text: Applies DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnostic algorithm CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale DSM-5) item scores returns single-column dataframe indicating diagnostic status. CAPS-5 uses 20-item structure, 0–4 severity scale, \(\ge\) 2 symptom-presence threshold PCL-5, diagnostic algorithm identical.

title: Compute CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnosis — create_caps5_diagnosis
text: data dataframe containing exactly 20 columns CAPS-5 item severity scores (output rename_caps5_columns). Columns must named symptom_1 symptom_20, scored 0–4 scale, missing values.

title: Compute CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnosis — create_caps5_diagnosis
text: data.frame one logical column one row per   participant: PTSD_caps5: CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR diagnosis

title: Compute CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnosis — create_caps5_diagnosis
text: DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria applied : Criterion B (Intrusion): \(\ge\) 1 items 1–5 severity     \(\ge\) 2 Criterion C (Avoidance): \(\ge\) 1 items 6–7 severity     \(\ge\) 2 Criterion D (Negative cognitions/mood): \(\ge\) 2 items 8–14     severity \(\ge\) 2 Criterion E (Arousal/reactivity): \(\ge\) 2 items 15–20     severity \(\ge\) 2 four criteria must met positive diagnosis. Unlike create_icd11_diagnosis, function returns CAPS-5 diagnosis column (PTSD_caps5), PCL-5 reference column. CAPS-5 diagnosis typically used gold-standard reference , compared PCL-5 baseline. returned dataframe can passed compare_diagnostic_systems via caps5_data parameter, used directly descriptive analyses.

title: Compute CAPS-5 DSM-5-TR PTSD diagnosis — create_caps5_diagnosis
code: # Simulate CAPS-5 data (using same structure as PCL-5) set.seed(42) caps5_raw <- data.frame(matrix(sample(0:4, 400, replace = TRUE), ncol = 20)) caps5_data <- rename_caps5_columns(caps5_raw) caps5_dx <- create_caps5_diagnosis(caps5_data) head(caps5_dx) #>   PTSD_caps5 #> 1      FALSE #> 2      FALSE #> 3       TRUE #> 4       TRUE #> 5      FALSE #> 6      FALSE table(caps5_dx$PTSD_caps5) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>     9    11

title: Apply ICD-11 PTSD diagnostic criteria to PCL-5 data — create_icd11_diagnosis
text: ICD-11 PTSD requires THREE following clusters met (symptom present = score \(\ge\) 2 original 0–4 scale): Re-experiencing (present): \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5     items 1, 2, 3 (intrusive memories, nightmares, flashbacks) Avoidance: \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5 items 6, 7 Sense current threat: \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5 items 16, 17     (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle) minimum 3 symptoms total across ICD-11 items (1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 16, 17) must present. automatically satisfied three cluster requirements met enforced explicitly clarity. DSM-5-TR diagnosis (PTSD_orig) computed using binarization logic rest package (create_ptsd_diagnosis_binarized).

title: Determine PTSD diagnosis based on DSM-5 criteria using binarized scores — create_ptsd_diagnosis_binarized
text: function applies DSM-5 diagnostic criteria PTSD using binary indicators symptom presence: Criterion B (Intrusion): least 1 present symptom items 1-5 Criterion C (Avoidance): least 1 present symptom items 6-7 Criterion D (Negative alterations cognitions mood):        least 2 present symptoms items 8-14 Criterion E (Alterations arousal reactivity):        least 2 present symptoms items 15-20

code: # Create sample diagnostic data set.seed(123) n_cases <- 100 sample_data <- data.frame(   PTSD_orig = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE),   PTSD_alt1 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE),   PTSD_alt2 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE) )  # Calculate diagnostic metrics diagnostic_metrics <- summarize_ptsd_changes(sample_data) diagnostic_metrics #>              column diagnosed non_diagnosed newly_diagnosed newly_nondiagnosed #> PTSD_orig PTSD_orig        57            43               0                  0 #> PTSD_alt1 PTSD_alt1        46            54              20                 31 #> PTSD_alt2 PTSD_alt2        51            49              26                 32 #>           true_positive true_negative true_cases false_cases sensitivity #> PTSD_orig            57            43        100           0   1.0000000 #> PTSD_alt1            26            23         49          51   0.4561404 #> PTSD_alt2            25            17         42          58   0.4385965 #>           specificity       ppv       npv diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig   1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000                57 #> PTSD_alt1   0.5348837 0.5652174 0.4259259                46 #> PTSD_alt2   0.3953488 0.4901961 0.3469388                51 #>           non_diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig                    43 #> PTSD_alt1                    54 #> PTSD_alt2                    49

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
text: Exports optimized symptom combinations human-readable JSON file. enables sharing derived combinations across research groups without needing share raw data, supporting reproducible derivation-validation workflows.

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
code: write_combinations(   combinations,   file,   n_required = 4,   clusters = NULL,   n_symptoms = NULL,   score_by = NULL,   description = "" )

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
text: combinations list integer vectors specifying symptom combinations, full result object optimize_combinations / optimize_combinations_clusters (contains $best_symptoms). file Character string. Path output JSON file. n_required Integer specifying many symptoms must present positive diagnosis (default: 4). value stored file read_combinations can retrieve . clusters NULL (default) non-hierarchical combinations, named list integer vectors defining cluster structure hierarchical combinations. Stored file use apply_symptom_combinations. n_symptoms Integer NULL. Number symptoms per combination. NULL (default), inferred length first combination. score_by Character NULL. scoring criterion used optimization ("false_cases" "newly_nondiagnosed"). Stored metadata reproducibility. NULL (default), omitted file. description Character string. Optional free-text description derivation context (e.g., sample characteristics, dataset name). Default empty string.

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
text: JSON file contains combinations alongside metadata needed apply via apply_symptom_combinations: required symptom threshold (n_required) optional cluster structure (clusters). Additional fields (score_by, n_symptoms, description) provide context reproducibility. combinations argument can either: list integer vectors (e.g., results$best_symptoms) full result object optimize_combinations     optimize_combinations_clusters (function automatically     extracts $best_symptoms)

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
code: # Create example combinations my_combos <- list(   c(1, 6, 8, 10, 15, 19),   c(2, 7, 9, 11, 16, 20) )  # Write to a temporary file tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") write_combinations(my_combos, tmp, n_required = 4,                    score_by = "false_cases",                    description = "Example non-hierarchical combinations") #> Combinations written to /tmp/RtmpZ8DwTn/file1aa53b940a1c.json  # Can also pass a full optimization result directly: # write_combinations(optimization_result, tmp, n_required = 4)  # Clean up unlink(tmp)

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.0
text: New generalized optimization functions optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() allow configurable number symptoms per combination (n_symptoms), required threshold (n_required), number top results (n_top), custom cluster definitions (clusters). New apply_symptom_combinations() function external validation: apply pre-derived combinations new dataset compare DSM-5 baseline. New write_combinations() read_combinations() functions exporting importing optimized symptom combinations human-readable JSON files, enabling collaboration across research groups without sharing raw data. Original functions analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required() analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required_clusters() preserved backward-compatible wrappers PCL-5 defaults. holdout_validation() cross_validation() now accept n_symptoms, n_required, n_top parameters. Internal refactoring: shared validation diagnosis logic consolidated reusable internal helpers.

title: PTSDdiag 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-02-13 Initial CRAN submission. Core analysis functions identifying optimal 6-symptom PTSD diagnostic combinations using PCL-5 data. Hierarchical (cluster-based) non-hierarchical analysis approaches. DSM-5 diagnostic criteria implementation (binarized non-binarized). Diagnostic accuracy metrics: sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV. Holdout k-fold cross-validation methods model evaluation. Simulated PCL-5 dataset 5,000 observations demonstration.
